/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-020.md:7184:- Questions answered: adopt only static reference integrity now; prototype freshness only with explicit trust semantics; build repair briefs as a new feature; defer inventory-backed parity until a cross-surface model exists; reject auto-growth defaults and reject score-first health
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-020.md:7187:- What worked: tracing Mex in execution order, then comparing each mechanism to current Spec Kit trust and health contracts, made the keep/defer/reject boundaries much clearer than the README alone.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:31:Research Mex's markdown scaffold and drift detection patterns to identify practical, evidence-backed improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`, especially around staleness detection, codebase-knowledge synchronization, and structured project knowledge. Determine which Mex patterns should be `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:50:`Code_Environment/Public` already has Spec Kit validation via `validate.sh` with severity-oriented checks, indexed memory files with frontmatter, and a `MEMORY.md`-style documentation/memory model. What it does **not** currently have is an automated drift checker that compares documentation or memory scaffolds against the actual filesystem, commands, dependencies, git staleness, or cross-file contradictions. Research should stay centered on that gap rather than re-covering general memory persistence already addressed by phase `001`.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:74:15. Save all outputs inside this phase folder, especially under `research/`. Every finding must cite exact file paths, describe what Mex actually does, why it matters for `Code_Environment/Public`, whether it should be `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`, what subsystem it would affect, and what migration or validation risk comes with it. When research is complete, update `checklist.md`, create `implementation-summary.md`, and save memory with:
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:116:`src/drift/checkers/staleness.ts` does not rely on human judgment alone; it flags scaffold files by both elapsed days and commits since last change. A strong finding would explain why combining temporal and repository-activity signals is stronger than date-only freshness, show how that could improve Public's documentation hygiene, and classify the idea as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:161:- findings that classify each recommendation as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`

/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:8478:    98	| Command | What it does |
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9222:### Finding 1: Mex’s real innovation is a separate knowledge control plane, not markdown storage alone
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9224:- **What it does**: Mex explicitly splits the system into `init` pre-analysis, `check` static drift detection, `sync` targeted repair, and `pattern add` scaffold growth. `ROUTER.md` then turns that into an operating loop: `CONTEXT -> BUILD -> VERIFY -> DEBUG -> GROW`. The important part is that markdown is not trusted by default; it is continuously audited and selectively repaired.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9225:- **Why it matters for us**: Spec Kit Memory is strong at retrieval, resume, trigger recall, and save-time quality, but it does not yet have a dedicated repo-wide “is our durable project truth still true?” plane. Mex adds that missing authority surface.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9226:- **Recommendation**: NEW FEATURE
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9227:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9229:### Finding 2: The hinge is claim extraction: Mex converts prose into typed, checkable assertions
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9231:- **What it does**: `extractClaims()` mines inline code, code blocks, bold dependency names, and version phrases into typed claims: `path`, `command`, `dependency`, and `version`. It deliberately skips template placeholders, HTTP routes, code snippets, and negated sections. The checker suite then validates each claim family against disk, frontmatter edges, manifests, and other scaffold files.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9232:- **Why it matters for us**: Our current guardrails validate memory file quality after write and recover session context well, but they do not parse repo guidance and durable docs into machine-auditable claims. That is the core mechanism we would need if we want automated doc-to-code truth checks rather than just structure checks.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9233:- **Recommendation**: prototype later
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9234:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9236:### Finding 3: Targeted sync is the strongest adoptable pattern because it repairs only the broken knowledge surface
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9238:- **What it does**: `runSync()` reruns drift detection, filters to error-bearing files by default, groups issues per file, and builds a combined brief. That brief includes the exact issue list, current file content, nearby filesystem context for missing paths, and a recent git diff for referenced paths. The repair instruction is explicitly bounded: change only what is necessary, then re-run drift and loop again.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9239:- **Why it matters for us**: This is the clearest gap in Spec Kit Memory. We have `memory_health`, `session_resume`, `session_bootstrap`, `post-save-review`, and `validate-memory-quality`, but none of them emits a minimal, evidence-rich remediation packet for stale repo guidance, spec docs, or memory-facing documentation. Mex’s repair loop is the most directly portable idea.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9240:- **Recommendation**: adopt now
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9241:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9243:### Finding 4: Mex’s freshness model is useful because it measures both elapsed time and repo churn
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9245:- **What it does**: Every scaffold file gets two staleness checks: days since last change and commits since last change, with thresholds of `30/90` days and `50/200` commits. That means a doc can be stale because the repo changed around it rapidly even if not much wall-clock time passed.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9246:- **Why it matters for us**: Our freshness signals today are mostly runtime recovery signals: session continuity, code-graph readiness, CocoIndex availability, database health. Mex suggests a new axis: “durable project truth freshness relative to repo activity.” That complements, rather than replaces, `session_resume` and `session_bootstrap`.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9247:- **Recommendation**: prototype later
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9248:- **Impact**: medium
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9250:### Finding 5: Mex also checks omissions, not just false statements, via reality-to-scaffold coverage
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9252:- **What it does**: `checkScriptCoverage()` loads `package.json` scripts, ignores lifecycle noise, and warns when a real script is not mentioned anywhere in scaffold text. That is a reverse audit: reality proves docs incomplete, even when docs never made a wrong claim.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9253:- **Why it matters for us**: Spec Kit’s current checks are stronger on write-time artifact integrity and runtime system health than on omission detection in durable repo guidance. A lightweight “undocumented operational surface” checker would add value, especially for scripts, commands, and perhaps major MCP surfaces. The caveat is that Mex’s implementation is intentionally shallow string matching, so we should copy the pattern, not the exact heuristic.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9254:- **Recommendation**: prototype later
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9255:- **Impact**: medium
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9289:### Finding 1: Mex’s real innovation is a separate knowledge control plane, not markdown storage alone

/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9300:- **Recommendation**: prototype later
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9301:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9303:### Finding 3: Targeted sync is the strongest adoptable pattern because it repairs only the broken knowledge surface
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9305:- **What it does**: `runSync()` reruns drift detection, filters to error-bearing files by default, groups issues per file, and builds a combined brief. That brief includes the exact issue list, current file content, nearby filesystem context for missing paths, and a recent git diff for referenced paths. The repair instruction is explicitly bounded: change only what is necessary, then re-run drift and loop again.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9306:- **Why it matters for us**: This is the clearest gap in Spec Kit Memory. We have `memory_health`, `session_resume`, `session_bootstrap`, `post-save-review`, and `validate-memory-quality`, but none of them emits a minimal, evidence-rich remediation packet for stale repo guidance, spec docs, or memory-facing documentation. Mex’s repair loop is the most directly portable idea.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9307:- **Recommendation**: adopt now
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9308:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9310:### Finding 4: Mex’s freshness model is useful because it measures both elapsed time and repo churn
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9312:- **What it does**: Every scaffold file gets two staleness checks: days since last change and commits since last change, with thresholds of `30/90` days and `50/200` commits. That means a doc can be stale because the repo changed around it rapidly even if not much wall-clock time passed.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9313:- **Why it matters for us**: Our freshness signals today are mostly runtime recovery signals: session continuity, code-graph readiness, CocoIndex availability, database health. Mex suggests a new axis: “durable project truth freshness relative to repo activity.” That complements, rather than replaces, `session_resume` and `session_bootstrap`.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9314:- **Recommendation**: prototype later
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9315:- **Impact**: medium
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9317:### Finding 5: Mex also checks omissions, not just false statements, via reality-to-scaffold coverage
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9319:- **What it does**: `checkScriptCoverage()` loads `package.json` scripts, ignores lifecycle noise, and warns when a real script is not mentioned anywhere in scaffold text. That is a reverse audit: reality proves docs incomplete, even when docs never made a wrong claim.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9320:- **Why it matters for us**: Spec Kit’s current checks are stronger on write-time artifact integrity and runtime system health than on omission detection in durable repo guidance. A lightweight “undocumented operational surface” checker would add value, especially for scripts, commands, and perhaps major MCP surfaces. The caveat is that Mex’s implementation is intentionally shallow string matching, so we should copy the pattern, not the exact heuristic.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9321:- **Recommendation**: prototype later
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-014.md:9322:- **Impact**: medium
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:1:### Finding 5: Mex’s registration model is small, explicit, and worth borrowing for a docs-drift subsystem
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:3:- **What it does**: Mex keeps the surface compact and explicit: one CLI entrypoint, one drift runner, one issue model, and three output modes. The checker pipeline is just a direct composition list in `runDriftCheck()`, not a hidden plugin system.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:4:- **Why it matters for us**: This is the reusable part. A Mex-like, source-of-truth validator for Public’s documentation and config surfaces would be cheap to reason about, cheap to test, and separate from the much heavier memory/retrieval stack.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:5:- **Recommendation**: adopt now
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:6:- **Impact**: medium
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8:### Finding 6: Public already has the large, layered surface that most needs Mex-style static drift checks
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:10:- **What it does**: Spec Kit Memory has a canonical 47-tool MCP registry, live `ListTools`/`CallTool` dispatch, a separate public API barrel for scripts, a separate maintenance CLI, and explicit runtime/client configuration guidance. But the surface is already drifting in docs: README says 47 tools while INSTALL_GUIDE still says 43.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:11:- **Why it matters for us**: This is the cleanest “adopt now” target from Mex. We do not need Mex’s storage model, but we do need Mex-like static checks over tool counts, named tool lists, command examples, and runtime config snippets so docs stop drifting from `tool-schemas.ts` and `context-server.ts`.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:12:- **Recommendation**: adopt now
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:13:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:50:Design the smallest Mex-style static surface checker for Public: validate tool counts, tool names, command examples, and runtime config snippets across [mcp_server/tool-schemas.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/tool-schemas.ts), [mcp_server/context-server.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts), [mcp_server/README.md](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/README.md), [mcp_server/INSTALL_GUIDE.md](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/INSTALL_GUIDE.md), and the checked-in client config files first. That is the highest-confidence “adopt now” slice from Mex for this repo.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:61:Research Mex's markdown scaffold and drift detection patterns to identify practical, evidence-backed improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`, especially around staleness detection, codebase-knowledge synchronization, and structured project knowledge. Determine which Mex patterns should be `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:80:`Code_Environment/Public` already has Spec Kit validation via `validate.sh` with severity-oriented checks, indexed memory files with frontmatter, and a `MEMORY.md`-style documentation/memory model. What it does **not** currently have is an automated drift checker that compares documentation or memory scaffolds against the actual filesystem, commands, dependencies, git staleness, or cross-file contradictions. Research should stay centered on that gap rather than re-covering general memory persistence already addressed by phase `001`.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:104:15. Save all outputs inside this phase folder, especially under `research/`. Every finding must cite exact file paths, describe what Mex actually does, why it matters for `Code_Environment/Public`, whether it should be `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`, what subsystem it would affect, and what migration or validation risk comes with it. When research is complete, update `checklist.md`, create `implementation-summary.md`, and save memory with:
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:146:`src/drift/checkers/staleness.ts` does not rely on human judgment alone; it flags scaffold files by both elapsed days and commits since last change. A strong finding would explain why combining temporal and repository-activity signals is stronger than date-only freshness, show how that could improve Public's documentation hygiene, and classify the idea as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.

Capture the evidence needed to prove description.json exists after create.sh; stale detection triggers on spec.md edit; per-folder files preferred over spec.md fallback; mixed-mode aggregation works; invalid JSON or schema-invalid description.json files are ignored, spec.md fallback is used, and existing files are repaired in place; missing description.json falls back cleanly without implicit backfill; out-of-base or prefix-bypass paths are rejected by realpath containment checks; YAML frontmatter is stripped before description extraction, including CRLF-heavy frontmatter cases; memory_context uses folder routing; regeneration leaves valid JSON on disk with no leftover temp files. Return a concise user-facing pass/fail verdict with the main reason.` | 1) Create spec folder via create.sh → verify description.json exists 2) Edit spec.md → verify isPerFolderDescriptionStale detects change 3) Run generateFolderDescriptions → verify per-folder files preferred over spec.md 4) Mixed mode: some folders with/without description.json → verify aggregation 5) Corrupt description.json with invalid JSON and schema-invalid field types → run generateFolderDescriptions() and verify spec.md fallback plus repaired description.json 6) Verify missing description.json falls back to spec.md without forcing a write 7) Attempt generation against an out-of-base or prefix-bypass path → verify rejection and no file written 8) Use spec.md with large YAML frontmatter and CRLF-heavy line endings → verify extracted description comes from post-frontmatter content 9) Run memory_context query → verify short-circuit folder routing | description.json exists after create.sh; stale detection triggers on spec.md edit; per-folder files preferred over spec.md fallback; mixed-mode aggregation works; invalid JSON or schema-invalid description.json files are ignored, spec.md fallback is used, and existing files are repaired in place; missing description.json falls back cleanly without implicit backfill; out-of-base or prefix-bypass paths are rejected by realpath containment checks; YAML frontmatter is stripped before description extraction, including CRLF-heavy frontmatter cases; memory_context uses folder routing; regeneration leaves valid JSON on disk with no leftover temp files | description.json content + stale detection output + folder routing trace in memory_context + recovery evidence for corrupt/schema-invalid description.json files + missing-file fallback evidence + rejection evidence for traversal attempts + proof of valid regenerated JSON with no leftover temp files | PASS: description.json created, stale detection works, per-folder preferred, mixed aggregation correct, no crash on corrupt description.json, invalid metadata repaired on regeneration, missing files fall back without implicit writes, traversal attempts rejected, frontmatter stripping works for CRLF-heavy files, folder routing active, and regenerated files are valid JSON with no leftover temp files; FAIL: Any of the scenario checks fails | Verify create.sh generates description.json → Check stale detection mtime comparison → Inspect generateFolderDescriptions preference logic and repair path → Confirm missing-file fallback does not backfill unexpectedly → Verify realpath containment rejects traversal/prefix-bypass paths → Confirm frontmatter stripping happens before description extraction → Verify memory_context folder routing |

/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8002:### Finding 1: Mex creates continuity by installing a scaffold, not by minting a session
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8004:- **What it does**: `mex setup` detects project state, optionally scans the repo into a brief, and launches or prints a population prompt. The next “fresh session” is verified by asking the agent to read `.mex/ROUTER.md`; there is no persisted session object, session ID, or bootstrap record created in setup.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8005:- **Why it matters for us**: This is a lightweight cold-start bootstrap pattern, but it is not equivalent to Spec Kit Memory’s actual session layer. Mex starts from scaffold availability; we start from a recovery surface with real state.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8006:- **Recommendation**: prototype later
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8007:- **Impact**: medium
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8009:### Finding 2: Mex survives across sessions by forcing every cold start through `AGENTS.md` -> `ROUTER.md` -> targeted context files
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8011:- **What it does**: Mex’s “session bootstrap” is really a reading contract. Every session must re-read the always-loaded anchor and router, then follow markdown edges into `context/` and `patterns/`. The `GROW` step updates those files so the next cold start inherits the latest project state. I infer from the inspected runtime types that Mex has no separate session-state model beyond this scaffold.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8012:- **Why it matters for us**: This is a strong navigational layer for stateless agents. It is also brittle: if the scaffold is not updated, session continuity degrades immediately because there is no fallback resume state.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8013:- **Recommendation**: adopt now
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8014:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8016:### Finding 3: Mex maintains context retrospectively with drift checks and repair loops, not live working memory
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8018:- **What it does**: `mex watch` installs a post-commit hook that runs drift checks after each commit. Staleness is judged by both days and commit counts. `mex sync` groups drift by file, builds a repair brief with file contents, filesystem context, and recent git diffs, then sends all flagged files to Claude in one interactive repair session and re-checks afterward.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8019:- **Why it matters for us**: This is not session memory in the conversational sense; it is a maintenance loop that keeps the scaffold trustworthy between sessions. That makes it a good complement to our system, especially for doc/config parity, but not a substitute for turn-to-turn continuity.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8020:- **Recommendation**: adopt now
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8021:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8023:### Finding 4: Mex has no explicit end-of-session summarizer, checkpoint, or trusted resume cache in the inspected runtime
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8025:- **What it does**: The exposed lifecycle commands are `setup`, `check`, `init`, `sync`, `pattern add`, `watch`, and `commands`. Even `sync`’s “one session” is just a transient Claude subprocess for repair. I did not find source-level constructs for `session_id`, continuation counters, summary caches, or explicit closeout artifacts in the inspected external runtime.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8026:- **Why it matters for us**: Mex can tell an agent where to look next, but it cannot natively answer “what did we do last session?” unless that answer was manually written into the scaffold. That is a hard limit for replacing Spec Kit Memory’s lifecycle features.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8027:- **Recommendation**: reject
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8028:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8030:### Finding 5: Spec Kit Memory already has the session primitives Mex is missing
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8032:- **What it does**: Our system exposes `memory_context`, `memory_match_triggers`, `session_resume`, and `session_bootstrap` as first-class tools. `memory_context` resolves caller-provided session IDs through a trusted server-side session manager and persists session state; `memory_match_triggers` rejects untrusted session IDs; `session_resume` adds a freshness-gated cached summary only when transcript identity and scope checks pass; `session_bootstrap` wraps resume plus health into a canonical recovery surface.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8033:- **Why it matters for us**: Mex’s markdown router can complement this, but it is operating at a lower level of fidelity. Spec Kit Memory already solves trusted resumption, health-aware recovery, and working-memory continuity in a way Mex does not attempt.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8034:- **Recommendation**: reject
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8035:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8037:### Finding 6: Spec Kit Memory also closes sessions explicitly through save-time summaries, continuation metadata, and learning records
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8039:- **What it does**: `generate-context` runs the workflow with structured session data and optional session IDs. `collect-session-data` builds explicit continuation artifacts such as `SESSION_STATUS`, `COMPLETION_PERCENT`, `PENDING_TASKS`, `CONTEXT_SUMMARY`, and `RESUME_CONTEXT`. The workflow links saved memories to predecessor sessions through `supersedes`/auto-causal links, and `task_preflight`/`task_postflight` persist learning deltas across a task lifecycle.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8040:- **Why it matters for us**: This is the piece Mex does not have at all: deliberate session ending and summarization. If we borrow from Mex, it should be for a thin markdown navigation layer over these richer saved artifacts, not for lifecycle management itself.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8041:- **Recommendation**: NEW FEATURE
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8042:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8096:### Finding 1: Mex creates continuity by installing a scaffold, not by minting a session
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8098:- **What it does**: `mex setup` detects project state, optionally scans the repo into a brief, and launches or prints a population prompt. The next “fresh session” is verified by asking the agent to read `.mex/ROUTER.md`; there is no persisted session object, session ID, or bootstrap record created in setup.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8099:- **Why it matters for us**: This is a lightweight cold-start bootstrap pattern, but it is not equivalent to Spec Kit Memory’s actual session layer. Mex starts from scaffold availability; we start from a recovery surface with real state.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8100:- **Recommendation**: prototype later
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8101:- **Impact**: medium
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8103:### Finding 2: Mex survives across sessions by forcing every cold start through `AGENTS.md` -> `ROUTER.md` -> targeted context files
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8105:- **What it does**: Mex’s “session bootstrap” is really a reading contract. Every session must re-read the always-loaded anchor and router, then follow markdown edges into `context/` and `patterns/`. The `GROW` step updates those files so the next cold start inherits the latest project state. I infer from the inspected runtime types that Mex has no separate session-state model beyond this scaffold.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8106:- **Why it matters for us**: This is a strong navigational layer for stateless agents. It is also brittle: if the scaffold is not updated, session continuity degrades immediately because there is no fallback resume state.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8107:- **Recommendation**: adopt now
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8108:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8110:### Finding 3: Mex maintains context retrospectively with drift checks and repair loops, not live working memory
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8112:- **What it does**: `mex watch` installs a post-commit hook that runs drift checks after each commit. Staleness is judged by both days and commit counts. `mex sync` groups drift by file, builds a repair brief with file contents, filesystem context, and recent git diffs, then sends all flagged files to Claude in one interactive repair session and re-checks afterward.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8113:- **Why it matters for us**: This is not session memory in the conversational sense; it is a maintenance loop that keeps the scaffold trustworthy between sessions. That makes it a good complement to our system, especially for doc/config parity, but not a substitute for turn-to-turn continuity.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8114:- **Recommendation**: adopt now
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8115:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8117:### Finding 4: Mex has no explicit end-of-session summarizer, checkpoint, or trusted resume cache in the inspected runtime
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8119:- **What it does**: The exposed lifecycle commands are `setup`, `check`, `init`, `sync`, `pattern add`, `watch`, and `commands`. Even `sync`’s “one session” is just a transient Claude subprocess for repair. I did not find source-level constructs for `session_id`, continuation counters, summary caches, or explicit closeout artifacts in the inspected external runtime.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8120:- **Why it matters for us**: Mex can tell an agent where to look next, but it cannot natively answer “what did we do last session?” unless that answer was manually written into the scaffold. That is a hard limit for replacing Spec Kit Memory’s lifecycle features.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8121:- **Recommendation**: reject

/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-004.md:8136:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-008.md:1:### Finding 6: Mex enforces repo-root and scaffold-root boundaries, but only at the project level
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-008.md:3:- **What it does**: `findConfig()` walks upward to the git root, refuses to run inside `.mex/`, errors when the scaffold is missing or incomplete, and reduces runtime state to `{ projectRoot, scaffoldRoot }`. Setup also refuses to run inside the Mex repository itself. This is a strong wrong-project guard, but not a multi-actor scope model.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-008.md:4:- **Why it matters for us**: the cheap guardrail is valuable. Compared with our `tenantId`/`userId`/`agentId`/`sharedSpaceId` model, Mex only scopes to a repo and scaffold. We should not downgrade to that model, but repo-root assertions are still a good low-cost hardening idea for doc-hygiene tools and wrappers.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-008.md:5:- **Recommendation**: adopt now
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-008.md:6:- **Impact**: medium
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-008.md:39:Research Mex's markdown scaffold and drift detection patterns to identify practical, evidence-backed improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`, especially around staleness detection, codebase-knowledge synchronization, and structured project knowledge. Determine which Mex patterns should be `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-008.md:58:`Code_Environment/Public` already has Spec Kit validation via `validate.sh` with severity-oriented checks, indexed memory files with frontmatter, and a `MEMORY.md`-style documentation/memory model. What it does **not** currently have is an automated drift checker that compares documentation or memory scaffolds against the actual filesystem, commands, dependencies, git staleness, or cross-file contradictions. Research should stay centered on that gap rather than re-covering general memory persistence already addressed by phase `001`.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-008.md:82:15. Save all outputs inside this phase folder, especially under `research/`. Every finding must cite exact file paths, describe what Mex actually does, why it matters for `Code_Environment/Public`, whether it should be `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`, what subsystem it would affect, and what migration or validation risk comes with it. When research is complete, update `checklist.md`, create `implementation-summary.md`, and save memory with:
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-008.md:124:`src/drift/checkers/staleness.ts` does not rely on human judgment alone; it flags scaffold files by both elapsed days and commits since last change. A strong finding would explain why combining temporal and repository-activity signals is stronger than date-only freshness, show how that could improve Public's documentation hygiene, and classify the idea as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-008.md:169:- findings that classify each recommendation as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`

/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-008.md:1864:.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/feature_catalog/19--feature-flag-reference/01-1-search-pipeline-features-speckit.md:133:| `SPECKIT_STRICT_SCHEMAS` | `true` | boolean | `schemas/tool-input-schemas.ts` | **IMPLEMENTED (Sprint 019, later expanded by session/code-graph additions).** P0-1: Controls Zod schema enforcement mode for the schema-backed L1-L7 MCP tool surface. /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-008.md:1887:.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/feature_catalog/14--pipeline-architecture/13-strict-zod-schema-validation.md:18:**IMPLEMENTED (Sprint 019, later expanded by session/code-graph additions).** The L1-L7 tool surface has Zod runtime schemas defined in `mcp_server/schemas/tool-input-schemas.ts` (re-exported via `tool-schemas.ts`), controlled by `SPECKIT_STRICT_SCHEMAS` (`.strict()` vs `.passthrough()`). Hallucinated parameters from calling LLMs are rejected with clear Zod errors and logged to stderr for audit trail (CHK-029). The L8 code-graph/CocoIndex dispatch helpers currently rely on lighter required-field guards rather than the same `validateToolArgs` path. Adds `zod` dependency.

When `true`, `.strict()` rejects unexpected parameters with stderr logging (CHK-029). When `false`, `.passthrough()` allows undocumented parameters for backward compatibility. The L8 code-graph/CocoIndex dispatch helpers still use lighter required-field guards instead of the same `validateToolArgs` path. |

/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-008.md:2638:.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/manual_testing_playbook/13--memory-quality-and-indexing/042-spec-folder-description-discovery-pi-b3.md:29:| 042 | Spec folder description discovery (PI-B3) | Confirm per-folder + aggregated routing | `Validate PI-B3 folder description discovery. Capture the evidence needed to prove description.json exists after create.sh; stale detection triggers on spec.md edit; per-folder files preferred over spec.md fallback; mixed-mode aggregation works; invalid JSON or schema-invalid description.json files are ignored, spec.md fallback is used, and existing files are repaired in place; missing description.json falls back cleanly without implicit backfill; out-of-base or prefix-bypass paths are rejected by realpath containment checks; YAML frontmatter is stripped before description extraction, including CRLF-heavy frontmatter cases; memory_context uses folder routing; regeneration leaves valid JSON on disk with no leftover temp files. Return a concise user-facing pass/fail verdict with the main reason.` | 1) Create spec folder via create.sh → verify description.json exists 2) Edit spec.md → verify isPerFolderDescriptionStale detects change 3) Run generateFolderDescriptions → verify per-folder files preferred over spec.md 4) Mixed mode: some folders with/without description.json → verify aggregation 5) Corrupt description.json with invalid JSON and schema-invalid field types → run generateFolderDescriptions() and verify spec.md fallback plus repaired description.json 6) Verify missing description.json falls back to spec.md without forcing a write 7) Attempt generation against an out-of-base or prefix-bypass path → verify rejection and no file written 8) Use spec.md with large YAML frontmatter and CRLF-heavy line endings → verify extracted description comes from post-frontmatter content 9) Run memory_context query → verify short-circuit folder routing | description.json exists after create.sh; stale detection triggers on spec.md edit; per-folder files preferred over spec.md fallback; mixed-mode aggregation works; invalid JSON or schema-invalid description.json files are ignored, spec.md fallback is used, and existing files are repaired in place; missing description.json falls back cleanly without implicit backfill; out-of-base or prefix-bypass paths are rejected by realpath containment checks; YAML frontmatter is stripped before description extraction, including CRLF-heavy frontmatter cases; memory_context uses folder routing; regeneration leaves valid JSON on disk with no leftover temp files | description.json content + stale detection output + folder routing trace in memory_context + recovery evidence for corrupt/schema-invalid description.json files + missing-file fallback evidence + rejection evidence for traversal attempts + proof of valid regenerated JSON with no leftover temp files | PASS: description.json created, stale detection works, per-folder preferred, mixed aggregation correct, no crash on corrupt description.json, invalid metadata repaired on regeneration, missing files fall back without implicit writes, traversal attempts rejected, frontmatter stripping works for CRLF-heavy files, folder routing active, and regenerated files are valid JSON with no leftover temp files; FAIL: Any of the scenario checks fails | Verify create.sh generates description.json → Check stale detection mtime comparison → Inspect generateFolderDescriptions preference logic and repair path → Confirm missing-file fallback does not backfill unexpectedly → Verify realpath containment rejects traversal/prefix-bypass paths → Confirm frontmatter stripping happens before description extraction → Verify memory_context folder routing |
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/iterations/iteration-008.md:2654:.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/manual_testing_playbook/13--memory-quality-and-indexing/133-dry-run-preflight-for-memory-save.md:29:| 133 | Dry-run preflight for memory_save | Confirm dry-run previews preflight plus semantic insufficiency without indexing side effects | `Validate memory_save dryRun preview behavior, including insufficiency detection. Capture the evidence needed to prove Dry-run returns preflight plus quality-loop and sufficiency payloads; thin memories report INSUFFICIENT_CONTEXT_ABORT without indexing/database mutation; force:true does not bypass insufficiency; rich non-dry-run save indexes the same file. Return a concise user-facing pass/fail verdict with the main reason.` | 1) Create a rich sandbox memory file and a thin sandbox memory file 2) `memory_stats()` and capture baseline totals 3) `memory_save({filePath:"<thin-sandbox-file>", dryRun:true})` and verify preflight details, `qualityLoop`, `sufficiency`, and `rejectionCode:"INSUFFICIENT_CONTEXT_ABORT"` 4) `memory_stats()` and `memory_search({query:"<thin title>"})` to verify no new indexed record from dry-run 5) `memory_save({filePath:"<thin-sandbox-file>", force:true})` and verify it still rejects before indexing 6) `memory_save({filePath:"<rich-sandbox-file>", dryRun:true})` and verify `would_pass:true` with no side effects 7) `memory_save({filePath:"<rich-sandbox-file>", dryRun:false})` and verify the memory becomes searchable | Dry-run returns preflight plus quality-loop and sufficiency payloads; thin memories report `INSUFFICIENT_CONTEXT_ABORT` without indexing/database mutation; `force:true` does not bypass insufficiency; rich non-dry-run save indexes the same file | Dry-run responses for thin and rich files + before/after stats + search outputs + non-dry-run save output | PASS if dry-run surfaces sufficiency explicitly with no index mutation, forced thin save still rejects, and rich non-dry-run save makes the record searchable | Inspect `handlers/memory-save.ts` dryRun and insufficiency branches, `shared/parsing/memory-sufficiency.ts`, `handlers/quality-loop.ts`, and `lib/validation/preflight.ts` |
