Capture hit, miss, eviction or invalidation evidence and return a concise user-facing pass/fail verdict with the main reason.`\\n/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/manual_testing_playbook/11--scoring-and-calibration/196-tool-level-ttl-cache.md:20:- Expected signals: first run records a cache miss for the tool/input combination; second identical run records a cache hit for the same SHA-256 key; cache stats reflect hits, misses, and invalidations; a relevant mutation or TTL expiry forces recomputation instead of returning stale results\\n/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/manual_testing_playbook/11--scoring-and-calibration/196-tool-level-ttl-cache.md:21:- Pass/fail: PASS: identical repeat work hits the cache within TTL and recomputes after targeted invalidation or TTL expiry; FAIL: repeat work misses unexpectedly, stale data survives mutation/expiry, or cache accounting contradicts observed behavior\\n/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/manual_testing_playbook/11--scoring-and-calibration/196-tool-level-ttl-cache.md:29:| 196 | Tool-level TTL cache | Confirm per-tool cache hits, TTL expiry, and mutation-driven invalidation | `Verify the tool-level TTL cache on a repeated expensive request. Run the same request twice within the active TTL window and confirm the second run is served from the per-tool cache using the same SHA-256 cache key. Then invalidate the relevant search path with a mutation or wait for TTL expiry and verify the next run recomputes instead of serving stale data. Capture hit, miss, eviction or invalidation evidence and return a concise user-facing pass/fail verdict with the main reason.` | 1) Confirm current cache settings, including active TTL window 2) Run a repeatable expensive request such as the same `memory_search` twice with identical inputs 3) Inspect cache stats or trace output to confirm miss then hit on the same tool/input key 4) Perform a relevant mutation such as `memory_save`, `memory_update`, or `memory_delete`, or wait past TTL expiry 5) Re-run the same request and confirm recomputation plus invalidation or expiry accounting | First run is a miss; second identical run is a hit; cache key is stable for identical tool+input; stats show hit/miss/invalidation activity; post-mutation or post-expiry run recomputes instead of returning stale data | Terminal transcript, cache stats or logs, repeated request output, and post-mutation or post-expiry rerun evidence | PASS: second identical run is a cache hit and the next run after invalidation or expiry recomputes cleanly; FAIL: repeated request misses inside TTL, stale results survive invalidation, or stats contradict the observed behavior | Verify TTL config and max-entry settings -> Confirm identical tool/input payloads were used -> Inspect cache-key hashing and per-tool scoping -> Check mutation hook invalidation path -> Review expiry cleanup timing and oldest-entry eviction behavior |\\n/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/manual_testing_playbook/11--scoring-and-calibration/197-access-driven-popularity-scoring.md:19:- Prompt: `Verify access-driven popularity scoring with two comparable memories. Repeatedly retrieve one target memory enough times to push the in-memory access accumulator past the flush threshold, then confirm the persisted access count and last_accessed fields update while a comparable control memory does not. Re-run a ranking query and confirm the more frequently accessed memory receives a stronger popularity contribution. Capture accumulator, flush, ranking, and dormancy evidence and return a concise user-facing pass/fail verdict with the main reason.`\\n/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/manual_testing_playbook/11--scoring-and-calibration/197-access-driven-popularity-scoring.md:20:- Expected signals: each retrieval increments the in-memory accumulator by 0.1; accumulated access flushes after the threshold is crossed; persisted `access_count` and `last_accessed` update for the hot memory; composite scoring reflects a popularity boost for the more frequently accessed memory; untouched control evidence remains comparatively colder for dormancy analysis\\n/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/manual_testing_playbook/11--scoring-and-calibration/197-access-driven-popularity-scoring.md:29:| 197 | Access-driven popularity scoring | Confirm batched access accumulation, thresholded flushes, and popularity-driven ranking effects | `Verify access-driven popularity scoring with two comparable memories. Repeatedly retrieve one target memory enough times to push the in-memory access accumulator past the flush threshold, then confirm the persisted access count and last_accessed fields update while a comparable control memory does not. Re-run a ranking query and confirm the more frequently accessed memory receives a stronger popularity contribution. Capture accumulator, flush, ranking, and dormancy evidence and return a concise user-facing pass/fail verdict with the main reason.` | 1) Prepare or identify two comparable memories that match the same retrieval query 2) Run the shared query once to establish a baseline ranking 3) Retrieve the target memory repeatedly until the accumulator crosses the flush threshold 4) Inspect accumulator state and persisted `access_count` plus `last_accessed` for target vs control 5) Re-run the ranking query and compare popularity-sensitive ordering and dormancy signals | Accumulator rises in 0.1 steps; flush occurs after the threshold is crossed; persisted fields update for the target memory; composite scoring or ranking reflects a popularity boost; colder control memory remains relatively less active for dormancy purposes | Baseline and post-access query results, accumulator snapshots, persisted access metadata, and operator transcript | PASS: target retrievals batch correctly, persistence updates are visible, and the hot memory gains a measurable popularity advantage; FAIL: accumulator does not flush, persisted metadata does not change, or ranking stays indistinguishable from baseline without explanation | Verify repeated retrievals hit the same target memory -> Inspect accumulator threshold and flush path -> Check `access_count` and `last_accessed` persistence writes -> Review composite scoring popularity contribution -> Confirm dormancy logic is reading updated access data |\\n/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/manual_testing_playbook/13--memory-quality-and-indexing/165-assistive-reconsolidation-speckit-assistive-reconsolidation.md:20:- Expected signals: similarity >= 0.96 returns 'auto_merge' and archives the older row; 0.88 <= similarity < 0.96 returns 'review' with AssistiveRecommendation logged; similarity < 0.88 returns 'keep_separate'; review tier produces classification (supersede/complement/keep_separate) without destructive action; companion merge guard aborts stale merge attempts with `predecessor_changed` or `predecessor_gone`\\n/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/manual_testing_playbook/13--memory-quality-and-indexing/165-assistive-reconsolidation-speckit-assistive-reconsolidation.md:21:- Pass/fail: PASS if auto-merge triggers at >= 0.96, recommendation logs for review tier, no destructive action occurs for borderline pairs, and predecessor-change detection blocks stale companion merges; /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:107124:/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/tool-schemas.ts:44:  inputSchema: { type: 'object', additionalProperties: false, properties: { input: { type: 'string', minLength: 1, description: 'The query, prompt, or context description (required)' }, mode: { type: 'string', enum: ['auto', 'quick', 'deep', 'focused', 'resume'], default: 'auto', description: 'Context retrieval mode: auto (detect intent), quick (fast triggers), deep (comprehensive search), focused (intent-optimized), resume (session recovery)' }, intent: { type: 'string', enum: ['add_feature', 'fix_bug', 'refactor', 'security_audit', 'understand', 'find_spec', 'find_decision'], description: 'Explicit task intent. If not provided and mode=auto, intent is auto-detected from input.' }, specFolder: { type: 'string', description: 'Limit context to specific spec folder' }, tenantId: { type: 'string', description: 'Tenant boundary for governed retrieval when memory_context routes to memory_search.' }, userId: { type: 'string', description: 'User boundary for governed retrieval when memory_context routes to memory_search.' }, agentId: { type: 'string', description: 'Agent boundary for governed retrieval when memory_context routes to memory_search.' }, sharedSpaceId: { type: 'string', description: 'Shared-space boundary for governed retrieval when memory_context routes to memory_search.' }, limit: { type: 'number', minimum: 1, maximum: 100, description: 'Maximum results (mode-specific defaults apply)' }, sessionId: { type: 'string', description: 'Optional server-issued session identifier for working-memory continuity. When provided, it must match an existing server-managed session or the call is rejected. Omit it to let the server generate a new session for this request.' }, enableDedup: { type: 'boolean', default: true, description: 'Enable session deduplication' }, includeContent: { type: 'boolean', default: false, description: 'Include full file content in results' }, includeTrace: { type: 'boolean', default: false, description: 'Include provenance-rich trace data (scores, source, trace) in results when underlying memory_search is called' }, tokenUsage: { type: 'number', minimum: 0.0, maximum: 1.0, description: "Optional caller token usage ratio (0.0-1.0)" }, anchors: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' }, description: 'Filter content to specific anchors (e.g., ["state", "next-steps"] for resume mode)' }, profile: { type: 'string', enum: ['quick', 'research', 'resume', 'debug'], description: 'Optional response profile formatter. Returns a reduced or mode-aware response shape when profile formatting is enabled.' } }, required: ['input'] },
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108263:### Finding 1: We should build a first-class `memory_sync_packet` workflow that turns drift findings into repair-ready briefs
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108265:- **What it does**: Mex groups issues by file, lets the operator sync errors first or include warnings, builds one combined repair brief, injects the current file content, issue list, nearby filesystem candidates, and relevant git diff, then re-checks in a loop until drift is resolved or the user stops. Our `memory_health` auto-repair is limited to DB/runtime integrity actions like FTS rebuild, trigger-cache refresh, and orphan cleanup.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108266:- **Why it matters for us**: We already detect many problems, but we still make operators manually translate diagnostics into fix plans. A repair-packet surface for stale memories/spec docs would turn health output into a low-token remediation workflow instead of a human reconstruction exercise.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108267:- **Recommendation**: NEW FEATURE
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108268:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108270:### Finding 2: We should add a durable project router surface with explicit “Working / Not Built / Known Issues” state
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108272:- **What it does**: Mex keeps a durable router file that re-grounds every session with current project state plus a task-routing table. Our startup surfaces are strong, but they are mostly transport/runtime summaries: graph freshness, session continuity, and next actions, not an explicit, durable model of product state and where work should route.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108273:- **Why it matters for us**: Retrieval answers “what memories match this question,” but not “what is currently true about this project.” A small, durable project-state board would reduce cold-start ambiguity and make stale-state drift visible in a way memory search alone does not.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108274:- **Recommendation**: NEW FEATURE
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108275:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108277:### Finding 3: We should create a first-class task-pattern registry instead of only storing episodic memories
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108279:- **What it does**: Mex distinguishes repeatable project playbooks from raw history. Patterns encode Context, Steps, Gotchas, Verify, Debug, and scaffold updates, and the system explicitly grows that registry after real tasks. Our save path captures rich session data and aggressively reviews memory hygiene, but it still produces memory artifacts, not reusable task playbooks.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108280:- **Why it matters for us**: We are good at remembering what happened. We are weaker at crystallizing “how to do this task correctly next time” into a durable, reusable unit. A pattern registry would convert repeated session knowledge into compact operator guidance and cut repeated rediscovery.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108281:- **Recommendation**: NEW FEATURE
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108282:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108284:### Finding 4: We should build a repo bootstrap brief generator for cold-start onboarding and scaffold seeding
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108286:- **What it does**: Mex builds a compact structured brief from manifest data, entry points, top-level folder categories, detected tooling, and truncated README content before asking AI to populate scaffold files. Our startup brief gives structural highlights and continuity, but not a comparable repo brief centered on manifest/tooling/README facts.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108287:- **Why it matters for us**: This would give us a low-cost “authoritative repo snapshot” for research, onboarding, and initial memory/scaffold seeding, especially in repos where no prior memory exists or where session continuity is thin.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108288:- **Recommendation**: NEW FEATURE
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108289:- **Impact**: medium
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108291:### Finding 5: We should add reverse coverage audits that detect live repo knowledge missing from memory/spec surfaces
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108293:- **What it does**: Mex checks the opposite direction from most doc lint: not just “does the scaffold claim something false,” but also “does the live repo expose scripts/commands/dependencies that the scaffold never mentions.” Our current quality checks are strong on saved-memory metadata, trigger drift, alias divergence, and DB integrity, but not on repo-to-knowledge coverage.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108294:- **Why it matters for us**: This would catch silent knowledge debt: new operational commands, dependencies, or repo affordances that exist in reality but never become retrievable memory or documentation. That gap is operationally expensive because nothing looks broken until someone needs the missing knowledge.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108295:- **Recommendation**: NEW FEATURE
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108296:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108357:### Finding 1: We should build a first-class `memory_sync_packet` workflow that turns drift findings into repair-ready briefs
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108359:- **What it does**: Mex groups issues by file, lets the operator sync errors first or include warnings, builds one combined repair brief, injects the current file content, issue list, nearby filesystem candidates, and relevant git diff, then re-checks in a loop until drift is resolved or the user stops. Our `memory_health` auto-repair is limited to DB/runtime integrity actions like FTS rebuild, trigger-cache refresh, and orphan cleanup.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108360:- **Why it matters for us**: We already detect many problems, but we still make operators manually translate diagnostics into fix plans. A repair-packet surface for stale memories/spec docs would turn health output into a low-token remediation workflow instead of a human reconstruction exercise.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108361:- **Recommendation**: NEW FEATURE
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108362:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108364:### Finding 2: We should add a durable project router surface with explicit “Working / Not Built / Known Issues” state
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108366:- **What it does**: Mex keeps a durable router file that re-grounds every session with current project state plus a task-routing table. Our startup surfaces are strong, but they are mostly transport/runtime summaries: graph freshness, session continuity, and next actions, not an explicit, durable model of product state and where work should route.

/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108446:- **Recommendation**: NEW FEATURE
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108447:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108506:Research Mex's markdown scaffold and drift detection patterns to identify practical, evidence-backed improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`, especially around staleness detection, codebase-knowledge synchronization, and structured project knowledge. Determine which Mex patterns should be `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108525:`Code_Environment/Public` already has Spec Kit validation via `validate.sh` with severity-oriented checks, indexed memory files with frontmatter, and a `MEMORY.md`-style documentation/memory model. What it does **not** currently have is an automated drift checker that compares documentation or memory scaffolds against the actual filesystem, commands, dependencies, git staleness, or cross-file contradictions. Research should stay centered on that gap rather than re-covering general memory persistence already addressed by phase `001`.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108549:15. Save all outputs inside this phase folder, especially under `research/`. Every finding must cite exact file paths, describe what Mex actually does, why it matters for `Code_Environment/Public`, whether it should be `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`, what subsystem it would affect, and what migration or validation risk comes with it. When research is complete, update `checklist.md`, create `implementation-summary.md`, and save memory with:
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108591:`src/drift/checkers/staleness.ts` does not rely on human judgment alone; it flags scaffold files by both elapsed days and commits since last change. A strong finding would explain why combining temporal and repository-activity signals is stronger than date-only freshness, show how that could improve Public's documentation hygiene, and classify the idea as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108636:- findings that classify each recommendation as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108646:- recommendation: `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108686:### Finding N: [Title]
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108688:- **What it does**: technical description with code evidence
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108689:- **Why it matters for us**: concrete relevance
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108690:- **Recommendation**: adopt now / prototype later / reject / NEW FEATURE
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:108691:- **Impact**: high / medium / low
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:109047:| Command | What it does |
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:112632:  // NFR-R01: Edge bounds — reject if node already has MAX_EDGES_PER_NODE auto edges
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:112636:      console.warn(`[causal-edges] Edge bounds: node ${sourceId} has ${edgeCount} edges (max ${MAX_EDGES_PER_NODE}), rejecting auto edge`);
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:112644:      console.warn('[causal-edges] insertEdge rejected non-finite strength');
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117852:### Finding 1: We should split our current “health” surface into storage health and knowledge-drift health
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117854:- **What it does**: Mex runs one explicit drift pipeline: discover scaffold files, extract typed claims, run per-file and cross-file checkers, then emit a single issue set with codes and severities. Our current architecture is split three ways: `memory_health` checks DB/provider/index integrity and auto-repair actions; `post-save-review` checks rendered-memory self-consistency; `validate-memory-quality` checks contamination and quality rules. Those are valuable, but they are not one coherent “knowledge truth” audit plane.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117855:- **Why it matters for us**: Right now a repo can be “healthy” at the storage layer while still having stale or incomplete operational knowledge. Mex’s strongest architectural lesson is not any single checker, but the separation of “is the store healthy?” from “is the knowledge still true?” We should stop stretching `memory_health` to imply both.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117856:- **Recommendation**: adopt now
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117857:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117859:### Finding 2: We should add a separate repo-activity freshness signal instead of overloading session/code-graph freshness
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117861:- **What it does**: Mex marks scaffold files stale on two axes: elapsed days and commits since last change. Our freshness story today is different: `session_resume` and `session_bootstrap` reason about code-graph freshness, availability, and resume context, while `memory_context` resume mode pulls memory search plus working-memory prompt context. None of those signals answer “has repo reality changed materially since this knowledge artifact was last touched?”
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117862:- **Why it matters for us**: This is a genuine refactor opportunity for our existing freshness model. Code-graph freshness is about structural index currency; session freshness is about conversational continuity. Neither should be treated as a proxy for documentation or memory freshness relative to repo churn.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117863:- **Recommendation**: prototype later
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117864:- **Impact**: medium
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117866:### Finding 3: Our repair architecture should emit structured issue bundles, not just hints plus internal auto-repair actions
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117868:- **What it does**: Mex does not stop at “warning” strings. It groups issues by target file, includes the current file content, the issue list, nearby filesystem evidence, and optional git diff context, then re-runs verification after repair. Our existing repair surfaces are mostly storage-oriented actions like FTS rebuild, trigger cache refresh, orphan cleanup, and generic next-step hints.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117869:- **Why it matters for us**: This is the deeper architectural version of the earlier repair-packet idea. The refactor is to make our diagnostics produce machine-usable repair context for knowledge surfaces, while keeping low-level auto-repair for storage problems. That would let bootstrap/health tools escalate from “here are hints” to “here is the bounded repair contract.”
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117870:- **Recommendation**: adopt now
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117871:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117873:### Finding 4: We should reject Mex’s heuristic markdown-claim extraction as a primary authority model
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117875:- **What it does**: Mex infers paths from inline code, commands from inline/code blocks, and dependencies from bold text under matching headings. That is clever and cheap, but it is still heuristic parsing of prose. Our current system already has more structured authorities: frontmatter, anchors, spec-folder linkage, and explicit rendered metadata checks.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117876:- **Why it matters for us**: If we copy Mex’s architecture too literally, we would move our strongest validation surfaces from structured metadata toward prose mining. That would be a step backward for a system that already depends on exact anchors, spec IDs, and render-path consistency.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117877:- **Recommendation**: reject
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117878:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117880:### Finding 5: If we add a pattern layer, it must be provenance-backed and quality-gated from creation time
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117882:- **What it does**: Mex’s `pattern add` creates a pattern file with placeholders, appends an `INDEX.md` row with `[description]`, and relies on later human/AI cleanup. That matches Mex’s lightweight scaffold philosophy, but it also means the system can knowingly create incomplete artifacts that immediately need another pass.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117883:- **Why it matters for us**: Earlier iterations already pointed toward a reusable task-pattern layer for Spec Kit Memory. The deeper conclusion here is that we should not copy Mex’s placeholder-first creation model. Our system already has stronger save-time and validation expectations, so any pattern subsystem should inherit those guarantees instead of bypassing them.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117884:- **Recommendation**: prototype later
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117885:- **Impact**: medium
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117887:### Finding 6: A single numeric “knowledge health score” could help operators, but only as a secondary surface
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117889:- **What it does**: Mex reduces heterogeneous drift into a top-line score and then still shows grouped issues by file. Our current system is richer on certainty labeling (`exact`, `estimated`, `defaulted`, `unknown`) and operational hints, but it lacks one fast “how bad is it?” number for knowledge drift.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117890:- **Why it matters for us**: This is worth considering, but only after the health split above. A score without a clean issue taxonomy would be noisy. Once storage health and knowledge health are separate, a score could help triage large audits or CI summaries.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117891:- **Recommendation**: prototype later
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117892:- **Impact**: low
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117952:### Finding 1: We should split our current “health” surface into storage health and knowledge-drift health
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117954:- **What it does**: Mex runs one explicit drift pipeline: discover scaffold files, extract typed claims, run per-file and cross-file checkers, then emit a single issue set with codes and severities. Our current architecture is split three ways: `memory_health` checks DB/provider/index integrity and auto-repair actions; `post-save-review` checks rendered-memory self-consistency; `validate-memory-quality` checks contamination and quality rules. Those are valuable, but they are not one coherent “knowledge truth” audit plane.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117955:- **Why it matters for us**: Right now a repo can be “healthy” at the storage layer while still having stale or incomplete operational knowledge. Mex’s strongest architectural lesson is not any single checker, but the separation of “is the store healthy?” from “is the knowledge still true?” We should stop stretching `memory_health` to imply both.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117956:- **Recommendation**: adopt now
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117957:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117959:### Finding 2: We should add a separate repo-activity freshness signal instead of overloading session/code-graph freshness
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117961:- **What it does**: Mex marks scaffold files stale on two axes: elapsed days and commits since last change. Our freshness story today is different: `session_resume` and `session_bootstrap` reason about code-graph freshness, availability, and resume context, while `memory_context` resume mode pulls memory search plus working-memory prompt context. None of those signals answer “has repo reality changed materially since this knowledge artifact was last touched?”
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117962:- **Why it matters for us**: This is a genuine refactor opportunity for our existing freshness model. Code-graph freshness is about structural index currency; session freshness is about conversational continuity. Neither should be treated as a proxy for documentation or memory freshness relative to repo churn.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117963:- **Recommendation**: prototype later
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117964:- **Impact**: medium
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117966:### Finding 3: Our repair architecture should emit structured issue bundles, not just hints plus internal auto-repair actions
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:117968:- **What it does**: Mex does not stop at “warning” strings. /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:118066:Research Mex's markdown scaffold and drift detection patterns to identify practical, evidence-backed improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`, especially around staleness detection, codebase-knowledge synchronization, and structured project knowledge. Determine which Mex patterns should be `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:118085:`Code_Environment/Public` already has Spec Kit validation via `validate.sh` with severity-oriented checks, indexed memory files with frontmatter, and a `MEMORY.md`-style documentation/memory model. What it does **not** currently have is an automated drift checker that compares documentation or memory scaffolds against the actual filesystem, commands, dependencies, git staleness, or cross-file contradictions. Research should stay centered on that gap rather than re-covering general memory persistence already addressed by phase `001`.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:118109:15. Save all outputs inside this phase folder, especially under `research/`. Every finding must cite exact file paths, describe what Mex actually does, why it matters for `Code_Environment/Public`, whether it should be `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`, what subsystem it would affect, and what migration or validation risk comes with it. When research is complete, update `checklist.md`, create `implementation-summary.md`, and save memory with:
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:118151:`src/drift/checkers/staleness.ts` does not rely on human judgment alone; it flags scaffold files by both elapsed days and commits since last change. A strong finding would explain why combining temporal and repository-activity signals is stronger than date-only freshness, show how that could improve Public's documentation hygiene, and classify the idea as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.

/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:124253:166:    status: 'rejected',

/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126414:    55	    ).rejects.toThrow("Invalid pattern name");
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126544:### Finding 1: Split “memory health” into storage integrity vs knowledge-truth drift
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126546:- **What it does**: Mex runs a separate static audit plane: it finds scaffold markdown files, extracts a narrow claim taxonomy (`path`, `command`, `dependency`, `version`), runs eight zero-token checkers, and emits a drift report independent of runtime state. Our `memory_health` focuses on DB/index/provider integrity, FTS5 consistency, orphan cleanup, and repair metadata, not whether durable guidance files make false claims about the repo.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126547:- **Why it matters for us**: The big shift is not “replace memory_health,” but add a second health surface for durable knowledge truth. Our current architecture is strong on retrieval/storage correctness; Mex shows we need a distinct control plane for doc/memory drift.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126548:- **Recommendation**: adopt now
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126549:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126551:### Finding 2: Freshness should include repository-activity drift, not just session and graph readiness
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126553:- **What it does**: Mex flags each scaffold file by both elapsed days and commits since last change, with warning/error thresholds at `30/90` days and `50/200` commits. Our freshness model is currently about session continuity, cached-summary age, and code-graph freshness.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126554:- **Why it matters for us**: Mex suggests a new freshness axis: “the repo moved, but the durable guidance did not.” That should extend our current freshness model, not replace it. Session freshness answers “can I resume safely?”; repo-activity freshness answers “is this durable guidance still believable?”
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126555:- **Recommendation**: prototype later
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126556:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126558:### Finding 3: Repair should be turned into bounded, file-scoped remediation packets
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126560:- **What it does**: Mex groups issues by file, then builds a repair brief that carries the current file content, exact issue list, nearby filesystem reality, and recent git diff before re-running drift check in a loop. Our current quality gates are strong at detection and blocking, but they mostly emit hints and severity findings rather than packaging a ready-made repair context for stale knowledge surfaces.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126561:- **Why it matters for us**: This is a strong architecture pattern for doc/memory remediation. Instead of “health says degraded” or “post-save review failed,” we could produce a bounded fix packet for the exact file or memory surface that drifted.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126562:- **Recommendation**: NEW FEATURE
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126563:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126565:### Finding 4: Durable project knowledge wants a doc-native routing scaffold beside indexed memory
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126567:- **What it does**: Mex assumes a tiny root anchor plus a router plus context/pattern files, then uses scanner-built briefs to populate those files from structured repo evidence. That is different from retrieval-first systems: it gives humans and agents a stable, navigable control surface before search starts.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126568:- **Why it matters for us**: Spec Kit Memory is already better at semantic retrieval, session continuity, and structural routing. The gap is a lightweight doc-native authority layer for stable project truths and task routing. Mex suggests this should complement indexed memory, not replace it.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126569:- **Recommendation**: prototype later
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126570:- **Impact**: medium
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126572:### Finding 5: Pattern growth is worth keeping, but only with a higher authority bar than Mex uses
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126574:- **What it does**: Mex treats post-task scaffold growth as a first-class loop and offers `pattern add` to create reusable task guides fast. But the default authoring contract is placeholder-first: new pattern files start with `[description]`, generic sections, and only minimal structural guarantees.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126575:- **Why it matters for us**: The paradigm shift is valid: reusable task guidance should compound from real work. But Mex’s implementation is too loose for our authority model. If we add a task-pattern layer, it should require evidence-backed creation, validation, and stronger metadata than Mex’s starter scaffolding.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126576:- **Recommendation**: NEW FEATURE

/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126644:- **Impact**: medium
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126699:Research Mex's markdown scaffold and drift detection patterns to identify practical, evidence-backed improvements for `Code_Environment/Public`, especially around staleness detection, codebase-knowledge synchronization, and structured project knowledge. Determine which Mex patterns should be `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126718:`Code_Environment/Public` already has Spec Kit validation via `validate.sh` with severity-oriented checks, indexed memory files with frontmatter, and a `MEMORY.md`-style documentation/memory model. What it does **not** currently have is an automated drift checker that compares documentation or memory scaffolds against the actual filesystem, commands, dependencies, git staleness, or cross-file contradictions. Research should stay centered on that gap rather than re-covering general memory persistence already addressed by phase `001`.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126742:15. Save all outputs inside this phase folder, especially under `research/`. Every finding must cite exact file paths, describe what Mex actually does, why it matters for `Code_Environment/Public`, whether it should be `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`, what subsystem it would affect, and what migration or validation risk comes with it. When research is complete, update `checklist.md`, create `implementation-summary.md`, and save memory with:
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126784:`src/drift/checkers/staleness.ts` does not rely on human judgment alone; it flags scaffold files by both elapsed days and commits since last change. A strong finding would explain why combining temporal and repository-activity signals is stronger than date-only freshness, show how that could improve Public's documentation hygiene, and classify the idea as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:126829:- findings that classify each recommendation as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, or `reject`

/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:135146:    98	| Command | What it does |
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:135890:### Finding 1: Mex’s real innovation is a separate knowledge control plane, not markdown storage alone
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:135892:- **What it does**: Mex explicitly splits the system into `init` pre-analysis, `check` static drift detection, `sync` targeted repair, and `pattern add` scaffold growth. `ROUTER.md` then turns that into an operating loop: `CONTEXT -> BUILD -> VERIFY -> DEBUG -> GROW`. The important part is that markdown is not trusted by default; it is continuously audited and selectively repaired.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:135893:- **Why it matters for us**: Spec Kit Memory is strong at retrieval, resume, trigger recall, and save-time quality, but it does not yet have a dedicated repo-wide “is our durable project truth still true?” plane. Mex adds that missing authority surface.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:135894:- **Recommendation**: NEW FEATURE
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:135895:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:135897:### Finding 2: The hinge is claim extraction: Mex converts prose into typed, checkable assertions
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:135899:- **What it does**: `extractClaims()` mines inline code, code blocks, bold dependency names, and version phrases into typed claims: `path`, `command`, `dependency`, and `version`. It deliberately skips template placeholders, HTTP routes, code snippets, and negated sections. The checker suite then validates each claim family against disk, frontmatter edges, manifests, and other scaffold files.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:135900:- **Why it matters for us**: Our current guardrails validate memory file quality after write and recover session context well, but they do not parse repo guidance and durable docs into machine-auditable claims. That is the core mechanism we would need if we want automated doc-to-code truth checks rather than just structure checks.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:135901:- **Recommendation**: prototype later
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:135902:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:135904:### Finding 3: Targeted sync is the strongest adoptable pattern because it repairs only the broken knowledge surface
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:135906:- **What it does**: `runSync()` reruns drift detection, filters to error-bearing files by default, groups issues per file, and builds a combined brief. That brief includes the exact issue list, current file content, nearby filesystem context for missing paths, and a recent git diff for referenced paths. The repair instruction is explicitly bounded: change only what is necessary, then re-run drift and loop again.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:135907:- **Why it matters for us**: This is the clearest gap in Spec Kit Memory. We have `memory_health`, `session_resume`, `session_bootstrap`, `post-save-review`, and `validate-memory-quality`, but none of them emits a minimal, evidence-rich remediation packet for stale repo guidance, spec docs, or memory-facing documentation. Mex’s repair loop is the most directly portable idea.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:135908:- **Recommendation**: adopt now
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:135909:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:135911:### Finding 4: Mex’s freshness model is useful because it measures both elapsed time and repo churn
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:135913:- **What it does**: Every scaffold file gets two staleness checks: days since last change and commits since last change, with thresholds of `30/90` days and `50/200` commits. That means a doc can be stale because the repo changed around it rapidly even if not much wall-clock time passed.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:135914:- **Why it matters for us**: Our freshness signals today are mostly runtime recovery signals: session continuity, code-graph readiness, CocoIndex availability, database health. Mex suggests a new axis: “durable project truth freshness relative to repo activity.” That complements, rather than replaces, `session_resume` and `session_bootstrap`.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:135915:- **Recommendation**: prototype later
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:135916:- **Impact**: medium
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:135918:### Finding 5: Mex also checks omissions, not just false statements, via reality-to-scaffold coverage
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:135920:- **What it does**: `checkScriptCoverage()` loads `package.json` scripts, ignores lifecycle noise, and warns when a real script is not mentioned anywhere in scaffold text. That is a reverse audit: reality proves docs incomplete, even when docs never made a wrong claim.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:135921:- **Why it matters for us**: Spec Kit’s current checks are stronger on write-time artifact integrity and runtime system health than on omission detection in durable repo guidance. A lightweight “undocumented operational surface” checker would add value, especially for scripts, commands, and perhaps major MCP surfaces. The caveat is that Mex’s implementation is intentionally shallow string matching, so we should copy the pattern, not the exact heuristic.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:135922:- **Recommendation**: prototype later
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:135923:- **Impact**: medium
