/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135581:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135583:### Finding 9: A native OpenCode memory plugin is viable only as a delegation-only facade
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135585:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne shows that a thin OpenCode wrapper can feel good, and Public already emits OpenCode transport plans from MCP-owned state.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135586:- **Why it matters for us**: If we build a native facade, it must delegate to MCP for save/search/recovery and keep CocoIndex/code-graph as separate retrieval planes, or it will become a second authority layer.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135587:- **Recommendation**: NEW FEATURE
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135588:- **Impact**: medium
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135590:### Finding 10: Optional offline backend readiness is worth prototyping, but Mnemosyne’s runtime stack should not become Public’s default
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135592:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne assumes a local binary plus a first-run model download and mainly reports failures through stderr or one install hint, while Public already has explicit bootstrap/readiness surfaces.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135593:- **Why it matters for us**: If we ever support optional local memory backends, they need a real warmup/doctor/readiness flow; Public should not switch its primary path to Bun-plus-binary-plus-model-download.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135594:- **Recommendation**: prototype later
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135595:- **Impact**: medium
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135613:- Executive summary: 10 consolidated decisions. `adopt now`: 2. `prototype later`: 2. `NEW FEATURE`: 2. `reject`: 4. The highest-confidence transfers are compaction-time memory action guidance and rewritten startup policy; the highest-confidence rejections are basename-derived scope, `core=true`, and Mnemosyne-style mutation semantics.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135634:FINAL SYNTHESIS: I consolidated the repeated iteration themes into 10 non-overlapping decisions so every substantive Mnemosyne finding lands in one bucket: `adopt now`, `prototype later`, `NEW FEATURE`, or `reject`.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135637:### Finding 1: Mnemosyne is not a retrieval-engine upgrade for Public
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135639:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne’s wrapper forwards search requests to a backend whose BM25/vector/RRF internals are only README-documented, while Public already runs a visible multi-stage hybrid search pipeline.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135640:- **Why it matters for us**: The transferable value is wrapper ergonomics, not ranking replacement, so using Mnemosyne as a reason to re-architect Public retrieval would be a category error.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135641:- **Recommendation**: reject
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135642:- **Impact**: high
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135644:### Finding 2: Compaction-time memory action guidance is the strongest adopt-now idea
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135646:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne preserves memory behavior across compaction with an explicit action card, while Public currently preserves continuity/state but not equally direct memory-tool verbs on the OpenCode surface.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135647:- **Why it matters for us**: A transport-owned, deduped memory reminder would improve post-compaction behavior without changing retrieval authority.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135648:- **Recommendation**: adopt now
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135649:- **Impact**: high

/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135653:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne repeats “recall early, save after decisions, delete contradictions” in AGENTS and compaction, but Public’s save/delete paths are governed and more constrained.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135654:- **Why it matters for us**: The behavioral pattern is good, but the wording must point to `session_bootstrap`, `memory_context`, governed saves, and careful deletions instead of cheap direct CLI mutations.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135655:- **Recommendation**: adopt now
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135656:- **Impact**: medium
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135658:### Finding 4: Read-only `project` / `global` recall aliases are worth prototyping
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135660:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne gives simple project/global tool pairs; Public already has richer runtime scoping via `retrievalLevel` and broader fallback behavior.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135661:- **Why it matters for us**: Thin read aliases could improve ergonomics, but only if they compile down to existing scope logic and do not invent new authority.

/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135667:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne normalizes the current directory basename into a collection key and auto-inits that collection on load.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135668:- **Why it matters for us**: That is fragile across renames, sibling worktrees, and same-name repos, and it is materially weaker than Public’s governed scope model.

/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135674:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne lets the agent store concise strings directly, while Public’s durable save path is JSON-primary, file-backed, preflighted, and governed.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135675:- **Why it matters for us**: A `memory_remember`-style helper could be useful, but only if it produces the same governed save artifacts rather than bypassing them.

/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135681:- **What it does**: Mnemosyne collapses “always-on” behavior into a simple `core` tag, while Public reserves that behavior for explicit constitutional-tier handling and budgeted cache/query logic.
/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:135682:- **Why it matters for us**: A shortcut would blur `constitutional`, `critical`, and ordinary memories and encourage over-promotion.

codex
The cross-phase `rg` sweeps are noisy because those reports are large, but they’re still surfacing the stable motifs: scoped identity, continuity ledgers, compaction guidance, repair loops, and hygiene truthfulness keep repeating. I’m tightening that into a smaller evidence set now and checking the validator output directly.

 succeeded in 0ms:
### Finding 1: Engram’s core store is a narrow relational spine, while Public’s memory DB is a much broader retrieval substrate
- **Source**: [store.go#L441](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/001-engram-main/external/internal/store/store.go#L441); [vector-index-schema.ts#L2299](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/search/vector-index-schema.ts#L2299); [vector-index-schema.ts#L1357](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/search/vector-index-schema.ts#L1357); [session-manager.ts#L959](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/session/session-manager.ts#L959); [checkpoints.ts#L1107](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/storage/checkpoints.ts#L1107)
- **What it does**: Engram persists a compact set of first-order entities: `sessions`, `observations`, `user_prompts`, FTS mirrors for observations/prompts, and sync tables. Public’s store starts from a much wider `memory_index` row with embedding state, governance scope, FSRS fields, quality metadata, chunking metadata, then layers `active_memory_projection`, `session_state`, and `working_memory` beside it.
- **Why it matters for us**: Engram’s schema is easier to reason about, but it is not richer than Public’s. Copying it wholesale would collapse capabilities we already rely on for hybrid retrieval, lineage, governance, and cognitive/session behavior.
- **Recommendation**: reject
- **Impact**: high

### Finding 2: Engram’s memory hygiene is row-level and overwrite-first; Public’s is lineage-first and append-first
- **Source**: [store.go#L948](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/001-engram-main/external/internal/store/store.go#L948); [store.go#L3201](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/001-engram-main/external/internal/store/store.go#L3201); [memory-save.ts#L937](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-save.ts#L937); [lineage-state.ts#L806](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/storage/lineage-state.ts#L806)
- **What it does**: In Engram, `AddObservation()` checks `topic_key` first and updates the same row in place with `revision_count++`; only if that misses does it do normalized-hash duplicate suppression via `duplicate_count++`. Public instead creates a new append-only row when content changes and wires it into lineage/projection state.
- **Why it matters for us**: The useful part is the stable thread identity, not the overwrite semantics. Engram optimizes for compactness inside one row; Public optimizes for provenance and recoverable version history.
- **Recommendation**: prototype later
- **Impact**: high

### Finding 3: Engram makes session and prompt history first-class durable data, which gives it a simpler continuity story than our split recovery path
- **Source**: [store.go#L441](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/001-engram-main/external/internal/store/store.go#L441); [store.go#L754](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/001-engram-main/external/internal/store/store.go#L754); [store.go#L1613](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/001-engram-main/external/internal/store/store.go#L1613); [mcp.go#L1030](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/001-engram-main/external/internal/mcp/mcp.go#L1030); [session-resume.ts#L4](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/session-resume.ts#L4); [generate-context.ts#L71](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/scripts/memory/generate-context.ts#L71)
- **What it does**: Engram stores `sessions.summary`, saves `session_summary` as a searchable observation, stores prompts in `user_prompts`, and builds startup context directly from recent sessions, prompts, and observations. Public recovery is more composite: `session_resume` merges memory context, code-graph state, CocoIndex status, cached hook summaries, and file-backed memories generated through `generate-context`.
- **Why it matters for us**: Public is stronger overall, but continuity is split across more layers. A small durable session/prompt ledger could reduce resume fragility after compaction or hook loss without replacing the current file-first save model.
- **Recommendation**: prototype later
- **Impact**: high

### Finding 4: Passive capture in Engram is intentionally lightweight and storage-native
- **Source**: [store.go#L3394](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/001-engram-main/external/internal/store/store.go#L3394); [mcp.go#L565](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/001-engram-main/external/internal/mcp/mcp.go#L565); [mcp.go#L1097](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/001-engram-main/external/internal/mcp/mcp.go#L1097); [generate-context.ts#L85](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/scripts/memory/generate-context.ts#L85)
- **What it does**: Engram looks only for the last `## Key Learnings`-style section, normalizes each item, dedupes by normalized hash, and saves each surviving learning as a `passive` observation. It does not run a separate review pipeline or build a special passive-memory schema.
- **Why it matters for us**: This is a good example of a low-complexity adjunct rather than a new subsystem. Public could add a narrow passive-ingest helper for subagent outputs or closeout text, but it should feed into the existing `generate-context` and quality-governed save path instead of bypassing it.
- **Recommendation**: prototype later
- **Impact**: medium

### Finding 5: Engram bakes replication intent directly into the storage layer through `sync_id` and mutation queues
- **Source**: [store.go#L450](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/001-engram-main/external/internal/store/store.go#L450); [store.go#L508](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/001-engram-main/external/internal/store/store.go#L508); [store.go#L585](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/001-engram-main/external/internal/store/store.go#L585); [main.go#L140](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/001-engram-main/external/cmd/engram/main.go#L140)
- **What it does**: Sessions, observations, and prompts all get stable `sync_id` values; `sync_state`, `sync_mutations`, `sync_chunks`, and `sync_enrolled_projects` track outbound/inbound replication state as part of the same SQLite database. That makes export/import/sync a storage concern, not just a CLI concern.
- **Why it matters for us**: Public has governance, checkpoints, and mutation/history helpers, but not this kind of explicit cross-machine mutation queue in the primary memory DB. If we ever want durable local-first sync, Engram’s queue tables are a concrete starting shape.
- **Recommendation**: NEW FEATURE
- **Impact**: medium

 succeeded in 0ms:
### Finding 1: Modus’s MCP health surface is thinner than advertised, and thinner than Public’s actual hygiene control plane
- **Source**: `external/internal/mcp/memory.go:7-39`; `external/internal/mcp/vault.go:219-227`; `external/internal/vault/vault.go:123-158`; `external/cmd/modus-memory/doctor.go:19-31`; `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-crud-health.ts:222-594`
- **What it does**: Modus exposes `vault_status` as its only MCP-visible hygiene/status tool in the memory toolset, and the tool description claims “file counts, index size, cross-ref stats.” But `StatusJSON()` only returns `total_files`, `index_docs`, and a top-level directory breakdown. The richer cross-ref counts are only computed inside the separate `doctor` CLI. Public’s `memory_health`, by contrast, reports database/connectivity state, embedding/provider metadata, alias conflicts, FTS drift, orphan cleanup outcomes, and gated `autoRepair`.
- **Why it matters for us**: For agent-facing hygiene, status needs to be both truthful and actionable. Modus splits diagnostics between MCP and an offline CLI and under-delivers on the MCP side; Public already has the stronger operational pattern.
- **Recommendation**: reject
- **Impact**: high

### Finding 2: Modus’s `doctor` has one reusable hygiene idea Public does not fully cover yet: content-level contradiction and duplicate-fact linting
- **Source**: `external/cmd/modus-memory/doctor.go:42-84`; `external/cmd/modus-memory/doctor.go:108-158`; `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-crud-health.ts:296-376`; `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-crud-health.ts:445-594`; `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/storage/causal-edges.ts:647-680`
- **What it does**: Modus `doctor` explicitly scans fact files for missing `subject`/`predicate`, duplicate `subject+predicate` pairs, and “same subject, same predicate, different value” contradictions. Public `memory_health` is stronger on infrastructure integrity and repair, but its current checks center on schema/DB/FTS/alias/vector/orphan health. Public’s contradiction tooling today is causal-edge oriented, not fact-content oriented.
- **Why it matters for us**: This is the clearest hygiene gap where Modus has a useful idea. A content-level contradiction/duplicate pass would complement Public’s existing infra health tooling instead of duplicating it.
- **Recommendation**: prototype later
- **Impact**: medium

### Finding 3: Modus cross-references are associative adjacency maps, not a causal-memory hygiene system
- **Source**: `external/internal/index/crossref.go:9-214`; `external/internal/mcp/vault.go:75-101`; `external/internal/mcp/vault.go:899-925`; `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/causal-graph.ts:339-540`; `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/causal-graph.ts:557-847`; `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/storage/causal-edges.ts:18-52`; `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/storage/causal-edges.ts:173-247`; `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/storage/causal-edges.ts:616-760`
- **What it does**: Modus builds an in-memory `bySubject`/`byTag`/`byEntity` cross-index, scores connections with fixed weights (subject `3.0`, entity `2.0`, tag `1.0`), and appends connected docs to search results or returns them via `vault_connected`. Public stores typed causal edges (`caused`, `enabled`, `supersedes`, `contradicts`, `derived_from`, `supports`), exposes traversal in both directions, tracks graph coverage/orphans, records `weight_history`, and applies contradiction detection and edge bounds.
- **Why it matters for us**: Modus’s cross-links are useful for loose discovery, but they do not preserve decision lineage or graph hygiene. They cannot answer the same class of “why was this decision made?” questions that Public’s causal graph can.
- **Recommendation**: reject
- **Impact**: high

### Finding 4: Modus has no trigger-recall plane; its query hygiene stops at query expansion
- **Source**: `external/internal/mcp/memory.go:7-39`; `external/internal/mcp/vault.go:21-104`; `external/internal/mcp/vault.go:273-343`; `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-triggers.ts:184-229`; `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-triggers.ts:273-550`; `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/parsing/trigger-matcher.ts:157-185`; `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/parsing/trigger-matcher.ts:337-440`; `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/parsing/trigger-matcher.ts:644-689`; `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/parsing/trigger-matcher.ts:695-791`
- **What it does**: Modus exposes search plus Librarian expansion, but no trigger-phrase cache, no prompt-signal vocabulary, no scope/session validation, no degraded-mode reporting for trigger sources, and no co-activation or tiered-context recall path. Public’s trigger system caches validated phrases from `memory_index`, uses Unicode-aware boundary matching plus candidate indexing, fails closed on scope errors, reports degraded source parsing, supports signal boosts, and layers session-aware decay/co-activation/tier filtering on top.
- **Why it matters for us**: Trigger recall is a hygiene/control feature as much as a retrieval feature: it governs safe latent-context surfacing. Modus does not offer a comparable mechanism to borrow here.
- **Recommendation**: reject
- **Impact**: high

### Finding 5: Modus’s markdown pipeline is intentionally permissive, but that permissiveness weakens authoritative memory hygiene
- **Source**: `external/internal/markdown/parser.go:98-159`; `external/internal/markdown/parser.go:161-186`; `external/internal/markdown/writer.go:10-51`; `external/internal/vault/facts.go:348-375`; `.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-save.ts:1273-1418`
- **What it does**: Modus treats malformed YAML frontmatter as raw body, skips parse failures during directory scans, writes frontmatter/body directly with no validation gate, and handles duplicate fact writes by suffixing filenames (`-2`, `-3`, ...). Public’s `memory_save` path runs explicit preflight checks for anchors, duplicates, tokens, and size, supports dry-run, and rejects invalid saves unless the caller deliberately bypasses preflight.
- **Why it matters for us**: Modus is optimized for lightweight human-editable vault ergonomics, not for high-integrity memory ingestion. That tradeoff is fine for a personal markdown vault, but it is weaker than Public’s current hygiene bar for durable indexed memory artifacts.
- **Recommendation**: reject
- **Impact**: high

 succeeded in 0ms:

## Findings
### Finding 1: Mnemosyne is not a retrieval-engine upgrade for Public
- **Source**: [external/src/index.ts:106](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/src/index.ts#L106), [external/README.md:82](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/README.md#L82), [memory-search.ts:482](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts#L482), [memory-search.ts:809](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts#L809); evidence: both
- **What it does**: Mnemosyne’s wrapper forwards search requests to a backend whose BM25/vector/RRF internals are only README-documented, while Public already runs a visible multi-stage hybrid search pipeline.
- **Why it matters for us**: The transferable value is wrapper ergonomics, not ranking replacement, so using Mnemosyne as a reason to re-architect Public retrieval would be a category error.
- **Recommendation**: reject
- **Impact**: high

### Finding 2: Compaction-time memory action guidance is the strongest adopt-now idea
- **Source**: [external/src/index.ts:210](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/src/index.ts#L210), [spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js:396](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/plugins/spec-kit-compact-code-graph.js#L396), [opencode-transport.ts:142](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/context/opencode-transport.ts#L142), [memory-surface.ts:640](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/hooks/memory-surface.ts#L640); evidence: both
- **What it does**: Mnemosyne preserves memory behavior across compaction with an explicit action card, while Public currently preserves continuity/state but not equally direct memory-tool verbs on the OpenCode surface.
- **Why it matters for us**: A transport-owned, deduped memory reminder would improve post-compaction behavior without changing retrieval authority.
- **Recommendation**: adopt now
- **Impact**: high

### Finding 3: Mnemosyne’s startup/AGENTS discipline should be reused only after translation into Public terms
- **Source**: [external/README.md:64](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/README.md#L64), [context-server.ts:755](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts#L755), [session-bootstrap.ts:163](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/session-bootstrap.ts#L163), [memory-save.ts:1136](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-save.ts#L1136); evidence: both
- **What it does**: Mnemosyne repeats “recall early, save after decisions, delete contradictions” in AGENTS and compaction, but Public’s save/delete paths are governed and more constrained.
- **Why it matters for us**: The behavioral pattern is good, but the wording must point to `session_bootstrap`, `memory_context`, governed saves, and careful deletions instead of cheap direct CLI mutations.
- **Recommendation**: adopt now
- **Impact**: medium

### Finding 4: Read-only `project` / `global` recall aliases are worth prototyping
- **Source**: [external/src/index.ts:118](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/src/index.ts#L118), [external/src/index.ts:160](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/src/index.ts#L160), [memory-search.ts:529](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts#L529), [memory-search.ts:818](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts#L818); evidence: wrapper-confirmed
- **What it does**: Mnemosyne gives simple project/global tool pairs; Public already has richer runtime scoping via `retrievalLevel` and broader fallback behavior.
- **Why it matters for us**: Thin read aliases could improve ergonomics, but only if they compile down to existing scope logic and do not invent new authority.
- **Recommendation**: prototype later
- **Impact**: medium

### Finding 5: Basename-derived project identity is too weak for Public’s memory authority
- **Source**: [external/src/index.ts:28](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/src/index.ts#L28), [external/src/index.ts:79](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/src/index.ts#L79), [external/README.md:55](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/README.md#L55), [memory-save.ts:1265](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-save.ts#L1265); evidence: both
- **What it does**: Mnemosyne normalizes the current directory basename into a collection key and auto-inits that collection on load.
- **Why it matters for us**: That is fragile across renames, sibling worktrees, and same-name repos, and it is materially weaker than Public’s governed scope model.
- **Recommendation**: reject
- **Impact**: high

### Finding 6: An ergonomic inline memory intake is valid only as a governed facade
- **Source**: [external/src/index.ts:138](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/src/index.ts#L138), [generate-context.js:85](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/scripts/dist/memory/generate-context.js#L85), [memory-save.ts:1128](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-save.ts#L1128), [memory-save.ts:1273](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-save.ts#L1273); evidence: both
- **What it does**: Mnemosyne lets the agent store concise strings directly, while Public’s durable save path is JSON-primary, file-backed, preflighted, and governed.
- **Why it matters for us**: A `memory_remember`-style helper could be useful, but only if it produces the same governed save artifacts rather than bypassing them.
- **Recommendation**: NEW FEATURE
- **Impact**: medium

### Finding 7: A direct `core=true` shortcut should not be added to Public’s main surface
- **Source**: [external/src/index.ts:140](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/src/index.ts#L140), [external/README.md:57](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/README.md#L57), [vector-index-store.ts:609](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/search/vector-index-store.ts#L609), [memory-crud-update.ts:67](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-crud-update.ts#L67); evidence: both
- **What it does**: Mnemosyne collapses “always-on” behavior into a simple `core` tag, while Public reserves that behavior for explicit constitutional-tier handling and budgeted cache/query logic.
- **Why it matters for us**: A shortcut would blur `constitutional`, `critical`, and ordinary memories and encourage over-promotion.
- **Recommendation**: reject
- **Impact**: medium

### Finding 8: Mnemosyne’s blind append and hard-delete lifecycle is the wrong primary mutation model for Public
- **Source**: [external/src/index.ts:147](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/src/index.ts#L147), [external/src/index.ts:193](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/src/index.ts#L193), [memory-crud-delete.ts:67](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-crud-delete.ts#L67), [memory-crud-update.ts:138](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-crud-update.ts#L138); evidence: both
- **What it does**: Mnemosyne exposes append-plus-delete behavior at the wrapper boundary, while Public wraps updates/deletes in transactions, history, ledgering, checkpointing, and hook invalidation.
- **Why it matters for us**: Public should keep its auditable mutation model and treat contradiction cleanup as structured revision, not as plain-text ID deletion.
- **Recommendation**: reject
- **Impact**: high

### Finding 9: A native OpenCode memory plugin is viable only as a delegation-only facade
- **Source**: [external/package.json:31](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/package.json#L31), [external/src/index.ts:38](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/src/index.ts#L38), [session-bootstrap.ts:330](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/session-bootstrap.ts#L330), [opencode-transport.ts:104](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/context/opencode-transport.ts#L104); evidence: both
- **What it does**: Mnemosyne shows that a thin OpenCode wrapper can feel good, and Public already emits OpenCode transport plans from MCP-owned state.
- **Why it matters for us**: If we build a native facade, it must delegate to MCP for save/search/recovery and keep CocoIndex/code-graph as separate retrieval planes, or it will become a second authority layer.
- **Recommendation**: NEW FEATURE
- **Impact**: medium

### Finding 10: Optional offline backend readiness is worth prototyping, but Mnemosyne’s runtime stack should not become Public’s default
- **Source**: [external/README.md:18](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/README.md#L18), [external/src/index.ts:67](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/external/src/index.ts#L67), [context-server.ts:751](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts#L751), [session-bootstrap.ts:194](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/session-bootstrap.ts#L194); evidence: both
- **What it does**: Mnemosyne assumes a local binary plus a first-run model download and mainly reports failures through stderr or one install hint, while Public already has explicit bootstrap/readiness surfaces.
- **Why it matters for us**: If we ever support optional local memory backends, they need a real warmup/doctor/readiness flow; Public should not switch its primary path to Bun-plus-binary-plus-model-download.
- **Recommendation**: prototype later
- **Impact**: medium

/bin/zsh -lc "sed -n '179700,179752p' /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md" in /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public
 succeeded in 0ms:
FINAL SYNTHESIS: Classify every major Mex mechanism as `adopt now`, `prototype later`, `NEW FEATURE`, or `reject`.

Executive summary: Mex is strongest as a thin markdown-integrity layer, not as a replacement for Spec Kit Memory. The clear win is narrow static reference integrity for markdown surfaces. Freshness is worth prototyping only if we preserve Spec Kit’s explicit `exact` / `estimated` / `unknown` / blocked semantics. Targeted repair briefs are worth building as a new feature. Inventory-heavy parity checks need a broader abstraction first. Auto-growth and score-first health should stay out.

## Findings
### Finding 1: Static markdown reference integrity is the only clear adopt-now slice
- **Source**: [README.md](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/README.md#L72), [claims.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/src/drift/claims.ts#L48), [drift/index.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/src/drift/index.ts#L17), [path.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/src/drift/checkers/path.ts#L8), [edges.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/src/drift/checkers/edges.ts#L5), [index-sync.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/src/drift/checkers/index-sync.ts#L6)
- **What it does**: Mex extracts typed markdown claims from inline code, code blocks, and dependency headings, then runs zero-token checks for missing paths, dead frontmatter edges, and `patterns/INDEX.md` drift. It already distinguishes real missing references from pattern/template placeholders by downgrading some path misses to warnings.
- **Why it matters for us**: This cleanly maps to our real gap. Spec Kit already has semantic retrieval, trigger recall, causal links, and health tooling, but it does not statically verify that markdown references still point to real files or that pattern indexes stay in sync.
- **Recommendation**: adopt now
- **Impact**: high

### Finding 2: Dual freshness signals are useful, but only as supplemental trust metadata
- **Source**: [staleness.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/src/drift/checkers/staleness.ts#L4), [git.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/src/git.ts#L12), [shared-payload.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/context/shared-payload.ts#L22), [session-bootstrap.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/session-bootstrap.ts#L219), [memory-triggers.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-triggers.ts#L329)
- **What it does**: Mex emits separate stale-file issues from both elapsed days (`30`/`90`) and commits since last change (`50`/`200`). If git history cannot answer, `git.ts` returns `null`, so the checker silently emits nothing.
- **Why it matters for us**: The days-plus-commits idea is strong, but silent `null` is too weak for Spec Kit. Our current surfaces already distinguish `exact`, `estimated`, `defaulted`, `unknown`, and degraded matching; freshness should plug into that trust model instead of pretending “no signal” means “healthy.”
- **Recommendation**: prototype later
- **Impact**: high

### Finding 3: Mex’s repair brief flow is worth building as a provider-neutral Spec Kit feature
- **Source**: [sync/index.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/src/sync/index.ts#L29), [brief-builder.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/src/sync/brief-builder.ts#L7)
- **What it does**: `mex sync` groups issues by file, builds one combined brief, includes the current markdown, issue list, nearby filesystem listings for missing paths, and `HEAD~5..HEAD` diffs for referenced paths, then runs an interactive repair loop and re-checks drift after each cycle.
- **Why it matters for us**: This is the best “AI-assisted” idea in Mex. It converts static failures into bounded, auditable repair work instead of re-running broad doc rewrites. That fits Spec Kit’s packet-local remediation style very well.
- **Recommendation**: NEW FEATURE
- **Impact**: high

### Finding 4: Command, dependency, cross-file, and script-coverage audits are good ideas, but Mex’s implementation is too Node-centric for direct adoption
- **Source**: [command.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/src/drift/checkers/command.ts#L5), [dependency.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/src/drift/checkers/dependency.ts#L38), [cross-file.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/src/drift/checkers/cross-file.ts#L3), [script-coverage.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/src/drift/checkers/script-coverage.ts#L26), [manifest.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/src/scanner/manifest.ts#L48)
- **What it does**: Mex validates npm/bun/yarn/pnpm scripts and Make targets, checks package dependencies and versions against `package.json`, flags version contradictions across docs, and warns if package scripts never appear in scaffold text. Non-Node manifests are mostly stubbed.
- **Why it matters for us**: We do need broader wrapper/config/doc parity checks, but our truth surfaces span shell commands, MCP tools, Python, TS, generated docs, and spec packets. Mex’s current inventory model is too narrow to trust in our repo.
- **Recommendation**: NEW FEATURE
- **Impact**: medium

### Finding 5: Scanner-first setup briefs are useful for bootstrapping, but not for durable truth maintenance
- **Source**: [README.md](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/README.md#L50), [SETUP.md](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/SETUP.md#L13), [scanner/index.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/src/scanner/index.ts#L20), [manifest.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/src/scanner/manifest.ts#L12)
- **What it does**: `mex init` builds a structured brief from manifest, entry points, folder tree, tooling, and README, then tells the AI to populate scaffold files from that brief rather than from raw browsing.
- **Why it matters for us**: This is good for first-pass scaffold seeding or packet bootstrap because it reduces exploratory noise. It is not good enough to become an authority layer for Spec Kit memory or documentation parity, especially with shallow non-Node support and no richer trust contract.
- **Recommendation**: prototype later
- **Impact**: low

### Finding 6: Mex’s GROW loop and `pattern add` workflow should not become part of Spec Kit’s default documentation lifecycle
- **Source**: [ROUTER.md](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/ROUTER.md#L56), [README.md](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/README.md#L178), [pattern/index.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/src/pattern/index.ts#L6)
- **What it does**: The contract requires post-task scaffold growth, and `pattern add` immediately creates a task-pattern template plus a placeholder `[description]` row in `patterns/INDEX.md`.
- **Why it matters for us**: In Spec Kit, partially formed docs become durable retrieval material quickly. Mex’s loop optimizes for compounding coverage, but it does so before truth is secured. That is the wrong default for a trust-sensitive memory system.
- **Recommendation**: reject
- **Impact**: medium

### Finding 7: Mex’s single drift score should not be promoted into Spec Kit’s primary health surface
- **Source**: [README.md](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/README.md#L87), [scoring.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/external/src/drift/scoring.ts#L3), [post-save-review.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/scripts/core/post-save-review.ts#L1019), [validate-memory-quality.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/scripts/lib/validate-memory-quality.ts#L45), [shared-payload.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/context/shared-payload.ts#L22)
- **What it does**: Mex starts at `100` and subtracts fixed costs per issue. That makes score the headline surface even though the underlying findings have very different trust implications.
- **Why it matters for us**: Spec Kit already has richer truth states: block-on-write rules, `REJECTED` post-save outcomes, and certainty/provenance fields. A single score would compress those distinctions and make unsafe or unknown states look deceptively acceptable.
- **Recommendation**: reject
- **Impact**: medium

7:### The highest-scoring AI memory system ever benchmarked. And it's free.
15:**The Palace** — Ancient Greek orators memorized entire speeches by placing ideas in rooms of an imaginary building. Walk through the building, find the idea. MemPalace applies the same principle to AI memory: your conversations are organized into wings (people and projects), halls (types of memory), and rooms (specific ideas). No AI decides what matters — you keep every word, and the structure gives you a navigable map instead of a flat search index.
17:**Raw verbatim storage** — MemPalace stores your actual exchanges in ChromaDB without summarization or extraction. The 96.6% LongMemEval result comes from this raw mode. We don't burn an LLM to decide what's "worth remembering" — we keep everything and let semantic search find it.
19:**AAAK (experimental)** — A lossy abbreviation dialect for packing repeated entities into fewer tokens at scale. Readable by any LLM that reads text — Claude, GPT, Gemini, Llama, Mistral — no decoder needed. **AAAK is a separate compression layer, not the storage default**, and on the LongMemEval benchmark it currently regresses vs raw mode (84.2% vs 96.6%). We're iterating. See the [note above](#a-note-from-milla--ben--april-7-2026) for the honest status.
32:[Quick Start](#quick-start) · [The Palace](#the-palace) · [AAAK Dialect](#aaak-dialect-experimental) · [Benchmarks](#benchmarks) · [MCP Tools](#mcp-server)
40:<td align="center"><strong>96.6%</strong><br><sub>LongMemEval R@5<br><b>raw mode</b>, zero API calls</sub></td>
46:<sub>Reproducible — runners in <a href="benchmarks/">benchmarks/</a>. <a href="benchmarks/BENCHMARKS.md">Full results</a>. The 96.6% is from <b>raw verbatim mode</b>, not AAAK or rooms mode (those score lower — see <a href="#a-note-from-milla--ben--april-7-2026">note above</a>).</sub>
52:## A Note from Milla & Ben — April 7, 2026
58:> - **The AAAK token example was incorrect.** We used a rough heuristic (`len(text)//3`) for token counts instead of an actual tokenizer. Real counts via OpenAI's tokenizer: the English example is 66 tokens, the AAAK example is 73. AAAK does not save tokens at small scales — it's designed for *repeated entities at scale*, and the README example was a bad demonstration of that. We're rewriting it.
60:> - **"30x lossless compression" was overstated.** AAAK is a lossy abbreviation system (entity codes, sentence truncation). Independent benchmarks show AAAK mode scores **84.2% R@5 vs raw mode's 96.6%** on LongMemEval — a 12.4 point regression. The honest framing is: AAAK is an experimental compression layer that trades fidelity for token density, and **the 96.6% headline number is from RAW mode, not AAAK**.
66:> - **"100% with Haiku rerank"** is real (we have the result files) but the rerank pipeline is not in the public benchmark scripts. We're adding it.
70:> - **96.6% R@5 on LongMemEval in raw mode**, on 500 questions, zero API calls — independently reproduced on M2 Ultra in under 5 minutes by [@gizmax](https://github.com/milla-jovovich/mempalace/issues/39).
72:> - The architecture (wings, rooms, closets, drawers) is real and useful, even if it's not a magical retrieval boost.
76:> 1. Rewriting the AAAK example with real tokenizer counts and a scenario where AAAK actually demonstrates compression
77:> 2. Adding `mode raw / aaak / rooms` clearly to the benchmark documentation so the trade-offs are visible
87:## Quick Start
111:## How You Actually Use It
126:### With Claude, ChatGPT, Cursor, Gemini (MCP-compatible tools)
133:Now your AI has 19 tools available through MCP. Ask it anything:
137:Claude calls `mempalace_search` automatically, gets verbatim results, and answers you. You never type `mempalace search` again. The AI handles it.
143:Local models generally don't speak MCP yet. Two approaches:
152:This gives your local model ~170 tokens of critical facts (in AAAK if you prefer) before you ask a single question.
169:Either way — your entire memory stack runs offline. ChromaDB on your machine, Llama on your machine, AAAK for compression, zero cloud calls.
198:Each wing has **rooms** connected to it, where information is divided into subjects that relate to that wing — so every room is a different element of what your project contains. Project ideas could be one room, employees could be another, financial statements another. There can be an endless number of rooms that split the wing into sections. The MemPalace install detects these for you automatically, and of course you can personalize it any way you feel is right.
200:Every room has a **closet** connected to it, and here's where things get interesting. We've developed an AI language called **AAAK**. Don't ask — it's a whole story of its own. Your agent learns the AAAK shorthand every time it wakes up. Because AAAK is essentially English, but a very truncated version, your agent understands how to use it in seconds. It comes as part of the install, built into the MemPalace code. In our next update, we'll add AAAK directly to the closets, which will be a real game changer — the amount of info in the closets will be much bigger, but it will take up far less space and far less reading time for your agent.
202:Inside those closets are **drawers**, and those drawers are where your original files live. In this first version, we haven't used AAAK as a closet tool, but even so, the summaries have shown **96.6% recall** in all the benchmarks we've done across multiple benchmarking platforms. Once the closets use AAAK, searches will be even faster while keeping every word exact. But even now, the closet approach has been a huge boon to how much info is stored in a small space — it's used to easily point your AI agent to the drawer where your original file lives. You never lose anything, and all this happens in seconds.
204:There are also **halls**, which connect rooms within a wing, and **tunnels**, which connect rooms from different wings to one another. So finding things becomes truly effortless — we've given the AI a clean and organized way to know where to start searching, without having to look through every keyword in huge folders.
218:  │    │  Closet  │ ───▶ │  Drawer  │                          │
233:  │    │  Closet  │ ───▶ │  Drawer  │                          │
239:**Rooms** — specific topics within a wing. Auth, billing, deploy — endless rooms.
240:**Halls** — connections between related rooms *within* the same wing. If Room A (auth) and Room B (security) are related, a hall links them.
241:**Tunnels** — connections *between* wings. When Person A and a Project both have a room about "auth," a tunnel cross-references them automatically.
242:**Closets** — summaries that point to the original content. (In v3.0.0 these are plain-text summaries; AAAK-encoded closets are coming in a future update — see [Task #30](https://github.com/milla-jovovich/mempalace/issues/30).)
243:**Drawers** — the original verbatim files. The exact words, never summarized.
252:**Rooms** are named ideas — `auth-migration`, `graphql-switch`, `ci-pipeline`. When the same room appears in different wings, it creates a **tunnel** — connecting the same topic across domains:
260:Same room. Three wings. The tunnel connects them.
267:Search all closets:          60.9%  R@10
273:Wings and rooms aren't cosmetic. They're a **34% retrieval improvement**. The palace structure is the product.
280:| **L1** | Critical facts — team, projects, preferences | ~120 tokens (AAAK) | Always loaded |
282:| **L3** | Deep search — semantic query across all closets | On demand | When explicitly asked |
286:### AAAK Dialect (experimental)
288:AAAK is a lossy abbreviation system — entity codes, structural markers, and sentence truncation — designed to pack repeated entities and relationships into fewer tokens at scale. It is **readable by any LLM that reads text** (Claude, GPT, Gemini, Llama, Mistral) without a decoder, so a local model can use it without any cloud dependency.
292:- **AAAK is lossy, not lossless.** It uses regex-based abbreviation, not reversible compression.
293:- **It does not save tokens at small scales.** Short text already tokenizes efficiently. AAAK overhead (codes, separators) costs more than it saves on a few sentences.
295:- **AAAK currently regresses LongMemEval** vs raw verbatim retrieval (84.2% R@5 vs 96.6%). The 96.6% headline number is from **raw mode**, not AAAK mode.
296:- **The MemPalace storage default is raw verbatim text in ChromaDB** — that's where the benchmark wins come from. AAAK is a separate compression layer for context loading, not the storage format.
348:# → 14 closets: OAuth refactor, dark mode, component library migration
431:- **Keeps a diary** — written in AAAK, persists across sessions
441:# → last 10 findings, compressed in AAAK
450:## MCP Server
467:| `mempalace_status` | Palace overview + AAAK spec + memory protocol |
468:| `mempalace_list_wings` | Wings with counts |
469:| `mempalace_list_rooms` | Rooms within a wing |
473:| `mempalace_get_aaak_spec` | AAAK dialect reference |
479:| `mempalace_add_drawer` | File verbatim content |
480:| `mempalace_delete_drawer` | Remove by ID |
496:| `mempalace_traverse` | Walk the graph from a room across wings |
497:| `mempalace_find_tunnels` | Find rooms bridging two wings |
504:| `mempalace_diary_write` | Write AAAK diary entry |
507:The AI learns AAAK and the memory protocol automatically from the `mempalace_status` response. No manual configuration.
534:Tested on standard academic benchmarks — reproducible, published datasets.
544:The 96.6% raw score is the highest published LongMemEval result requiring no API key, no cloud, and no LLM at any stage.
552:| **MemPalace (raw)** | **96.6%** | **None** | **Free** |

584:mempalace compress --wing myapp                   # AAAK compress

codex
The MemPalace side is very clear now: the unique pieces are the browse-first taxonomy, enforced hook timing, a protocol-teaching status call, and the explicit split between raw drawers and temporal facts. I’m pulling the exact Public comparison points next so the recommendations stay concrete and don’t flatten important differences.

### Finding 5: A separate temporal fact plane is still the biggest upside item, but it needs stronger authority controls than MemPalace has today
- **Source**: [knowledge_graph.py](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/005-mempalace/external/mempalace/knowledge_graph.py#L56), [knowledge_graph.py](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/005-mempalace/external/mempalace/knowledge_graph.py#L121), [knowledge_graph.py](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/005-mempalace/external/mempalace/knowledge_graph.py#L180), [mcp_server.py](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/005-mempalace/external/mempalace/mcp_server.py#L422), [README.md](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/005-mempalace/external/README.md#L64)
- **What it does**: MemPalace keeps `entities` and `triples` in SQLite with `valid_from` and `valid_to`, and it exposes query, timeline, add, and invalidate operations. But `kg_add` accepts uncited writes, contradiction handling is explicitly not wired into KG ops, and invalidation updates any matching live triple without a stronger review gate.
- **Why it matters for us**: Public genuinely lacks this architecture today. It is the clearest long-term differentiator from ordinary memory search, but it must be built with mandatory citations, authority tiers, correction review, and fact-versus-verbatim separation from day one.
- **Recommendation**: NEW FEATURE
- **Impact**: high

### Finding 6: Full palace taxonomy is still the easiest thing to overbuild and the hardest thing to keep honest
- **Source**: [room_detector_local.py](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/005-mempalace/external/mempalace/room_detector_local.py#L18), [miner.py](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/005-mempalace/external/mempalace/miner.py#L278), [palace_graph.py](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/005-mempalace/external/mempalace/palace_graph.py#L33), [README.md](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/005-mempalace/external/README.md#L62), [README.md](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/005-mempalace/external/README.md#L273), [BENCHMARKS.md](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/005-mempalace/external/benchmarks/BENCHMARKS.md#L602)
- **What it does**: Room assignment is driven by folder-name maps, filename/path matches, and keyword counts; the “graph” is then reconstructed from shared metadata. The repo’s own correction note says the celebrated boost was mostly metadata filtering, and the benchmark notes show room routing still behind the stronger no-rooms hybrid baseline.
- **Why it matters for us**: Public would pay for label governance, room drift, reindex churn, and false routing while duplicating capabilities it already gets from spec scoping, typed retrieval, and graph/code-search surfaces.
- **Recommendation**: reject
- **Impact**: low
